text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY
FOLLOW-UP VISIT -- ESTABLISHED PATIENT

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
FELLOW: [GU Oncology Fellow]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 67-year-old male with newly diagnosed metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (mCSPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, presenting for 3-month follow-up after initiation of androgen deprivation therapy plus abiraterone/prednisone. He comes in today primarily to review his PSA response and assess tolerability of treatment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old Polish-American gentleman and retired steelworker who was diagnosed approximately 3 months ago with de novo metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer. His presentation was notable for a PSA of 187 ng/mL, and staging workup revealed high-volume disease with 8 discrete osseous metastases (L2, T9, right acetabulum, right femoral neck, left iliac wing, right 4th rib, left 6th and 7th ribs) and bilateral pelvic lymphadenopathy (right obturator 1.8 cm, left external iliac 1.4 cm). Biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion. Comprehensive molecular profiling (FoundationOne CDx) was notable for a pathogenic germline BRCA2 p.E1308* nonsense mutation confirmed on blood testing, as well as somatic PTEN homozygous deletion, TP53 p.R248Q, RB1 loss, and CDK12 frameshift. SPOP p.F133V was also identified. MSS, TMB-low. Germline genetic counseling was initiated.

Given his ECOG 1 performance status, high-volume disease, and overall fitness, the patient was started on LHRH agonist therapy with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months in combination with abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally daily and prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily, consistent with the LATITUDE and STAMPEDE trial data supporting intensified therapy in mCSPC. Docetaxel was discussed as an alternative intensification option; however, after detailed discussion of risk/benefit, including his CKD (baseline creatinine 1.4, eGFR ~52) and patient preference to avoid IV chemotherapy at this time, the combination of ADT + abiraterone was selected.

Today he returns for his first on-treatment follow-up visit. He reports that overall he is tolerating the regimen reasonably well. He notes:

- FATIGUE: Present, Grade 1. He describes feeling more tired than his baseline, particularly in the late afternoon. He is still completing his activities of daily living and light household tasks independently. He is not experiencing daytime somnolence.

- HOT FLASHES: Grade 2. He is having approximately 6-8 episodes per day, sometimes interrupting sleep. He is aware this is expected with androgen deprivation. He has tried layering clothing. He has not yet tried pharmacologic management and asks about options.

- LOWER EXTREMITY EDEMA: Grade 1 bilateral ankle and lower leg swelling noted by patient, more pronounced in the evenings. He attributes it partially to sitting more due to fatigue. He has not had skin changes, warmth, or unilateral worsening to suggest DVT.

- PAIN: Denies any new or significantly worsened bone pain. He previously had vague low back discomfort at presentation that has improved. No new sites of skeletal pain. No radicular pain or neurological symptoms.

- URINARY SYMPTOMS: Mild nocturia x2 (unchanged from pre-treatment). No obstructive symptoms. No hematuria.

- GASTROINTESTINAL: Mild constipation managed with MiraLAX daily. No nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. No abdominal pain. He is aware of the mineralocorticoid excess side effects of abiraterone and has been watching for fluid retention.

- CARDIOVASCULAR: No chest pain or palpitations. Blood pressure has been measured at home 2-3 times/week per instruction; readings ranging from 118-130/72-80 mmHg. His antihypertensives were adjusted (lisinopril dose reduced) given excellent BP control, likely reflecting improved blood pressure dynamics in the setting of starting treatment.

- MOOD/COGNITION: Reports mild emotional lability and occasional irritability, which he attributes to hormone changes. Wife confirms. No frank depression or suicidal ideation. No significant cognitive difficulty at this time.

- APPETITE/WEIGHT: Appetite preserved. Slight weight gain of approximately 3 lbs, attributed to fluid retention and reduced activity.

He reports full compliance with abiraterone on an empty stomach (takes in the morning, at least 1 hour before or 2 hours after eating). He understands the importance of taking prednisone regularly with abiraterone to mitigate mineralocorticoid excess.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: fatigue, hot flashes, lower extremity edema, mild constipation, nocturia, mild mood lability.
Negative: no fever, no chills, no significant weight loss, no chest pain, no dyspnea, no new bone pain, no neurological symptoms (no weakness, no numbness, no bowel/bladder incontinence), no hematuria, no abdominal pain, no nausea/vomiting, no skin rash at this time.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, cT3b N1 M1b, diagnosed approximately 3 months ago (as above)
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- on metformin; baseline HbA1c monitoring in place; note that prednisone may affect glycemic control and will require close monitoring
3. Hypertension -- on lisinopril and amlodipine; dose adjustments as noted above
4. Mild chronic kidney disease, Stage 3a (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, eGFR ~52)
5. Benign prostatic hyperplasia (pre-existing, now in context of prostate cancer)

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Retired steelworker. Married, lives with wife in their home. Two adult children who are supportive and aware of diagnosis. Smoking history: 45 pack-years, quit at age 62. Denies current alcohol use (previously social drinker, self-discontinued). No illicit drug use. Ambulates without assistive device. Has not been able to perform his previous regular light walking due to fatigue but is motivated to maintain activity.

FAMILY HISTORY:
Brother: pancreatic cancer, deceased age 58.
Father: prostate cancer, age 74 (survived).
Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant identified in patient; family members have been informed and cascade testing has been recommended.

ALLERGIES:
NKDA (No Known Drug Allergies)

MEDICATIONS:
1. Leuprolide acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months -- last injection given today (second injection)
2. Abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg orally once daily on empty stomach
3. Prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily (with abiraterone)
4. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (with meals)
5. Lisinopril 5 mg orally once daily (dose reduced from 10 mg at prior visit given excellent BP control)
6. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily
7. MiraLAX (polyethylene glycol) 17 g orally once daily PRN constipation
8. Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily (per primary care)
9. Vitamin D3 2000 IU orally once daily
10. Calcium carbonate 1200 mg orally once daily (skeletal health in context of ADT)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 124/76 mmHg, heart rate 68 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16, temperature 36.8 degrees C, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight: 192 lbs (up from 189 lbs at baseline visit 3 months ago). BMI: 27.4 kg/m2.

General: Well-appearing, mildly fatigued-looking male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational and appropriate.

Head and Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear. No cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy palpable.

Chest/Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Normal respiratory effort.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated. No palpable abdominal masses.

Genitourinary: Deferred digital rectal exam at this visit given recent treatment initiation; previously documented T3b disease bilaterally.

Extremities: 1+ bilateral lower extremity pitting edema, symmetric, to the mid-shin. No erythema or warmth. Peripheral pulses intact.

Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Strength 5/5 bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. No focal deficits. Gait steady.

Musculoskeletal: No new tenderness to palpation over the spine, pelvis, or ribs. No focal bony tenderness elicited at the right femoral neck, left iliac wing, or lumbar spine (previously identified metastatic sites).

Skin: No rash. No gynecomastia noted at this time though patient was counseled to anticipate this with prolonged ADT. No injection site reaction from leuprolide.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
(Drawn this visit, results reviewed in clinic)

PSA: 4.2 ng/mL (down from 187 ng/mL at diagnosis 3 months ago -- 97.8% decline)
Testosterone: 18 ng/dL (castrate level achieved; goal <50 ng/dL)
LH/FSH: Suppressed (consistent with LHRH agonist effect)

Complete Blood Count:
- Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL (decreased from 13.2 g/dL at baseline; mild anemia, Grade 1)
- Hematocrit: 35.2%
- MCV: 88 fL (normocytic)
- Platelets: 212,000/uL
- WBC: 7.4 K/uL, ANC: 4.6 K/uL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.2 mEq/L (reassuring; monitoring for hypokalemia on abiraterone/prednisone)
- Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 1.4 mg/dL (eGFR ~52, stable at CKD baseline)
- BUN: 22 mg/dL
- Glucose: 142 mg/dL (mildly elevated; glycemic monitoring ongoing; HbA1c 7.4% at last check)
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
- AST: 28 U/L
- ALT: 32 U/L
- Alkaline phosphatase: 98 U/L (improved from 187 U/L at baseline -- reflecting osseous response)
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL

LDH: 168 U/L (within normal limits)
Magnesium: 1.9 mg/dL
Phosphorus: 3.4 mg/dL

HbA1c: 7.6% (slightly above target; will notify primary care for metformin dose optimization consideration; note prednisone contribution)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
No imaging performed at today's visit. Prior staging studies (bone scan and CT C/A/P at diagnosis, approximately 3 months ago) demonstrated 8 osseous metastases and bilateral pelvic lymphadenopathy as documented in the medical record. Restaging imaging planned at approximately 6 months of treatment per institutional protocol, given excellent PSA response.

---

ASSESSMENT:

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-SENSITIVE PROSTATE CANCER (mCSPC), HIGH-VOLUME, GLEASON 9 (5+4) GRADE GROUP 5, WITH GERMLINE BRCA2 MUTATION -- EXCELLENT EARLY BIOCHEMICAL RESPONSE TO ADT + ABIRATERONE

Mr. Kowalski has had a dramatic and gratifying response to intensified androgen pathway therapy. His PSA has fallen from 187 ng/mL at diagnosis to 4.2 ng/mL today (97.8% reduction) at 3 months. Testosterone is confirmed castrate at 18 ng/dL. The alkaline phosphatase, a useful surrogate for bone turnover in the setting of skeletal metastases, has normalized from 187 to 98 U/L, suggesting meaningful osseous response. These findings are consistent with the response patterns observed in LATITUDE and STAMPEDE, where combination ADT + abiraterone substantially improved outcomes over ADT alone in high-risk/high-volume mCSPC.

It is worth noting that his molecular profile is sobering: Gleason 9, BRCA2 germline loss, PTEN deletion, RB1 loss, TP53 mutation, and CDK12 frameshift collectively define a highly aggressive tumor biology with increased risk for early castration resistance and neuroendocrine transdifferentiation over time. We will maintain a low threshold for repeat imaging and biopsy should atypical progression patterns emerge.

The current PSA nadir trajectory is promising. We will continue the regimen and plan formal restaging at approximately 6 months.

2. TREATMENT-RELATED SIDE EFFECTS -- MANAGEABLE AT THIS TIME

- Fatigue (Grade 1): Consistent with ADT effect. Counseled on regular low-intensity physical activity, which has evidence for mitigation of ADT-related fatigue. Referral to cancer rehabilitation/exercise program discussed and offered.

- Hot flashes (Grade 2): Discussed pharmacologic options including venlafaxine or gabapentin if symptoms worsen or significantly affect sleep. Patient will trial behavioral approaches (loose clothing, cooler sleeping environment) for now.

- Lower extremity edema (Grade 1): Attributed to abiraterone mineralocorticoid effects combined with reduced mobility. Potassium confirmed normal. Patient instructed on leg elevation, compression socks. Weight is being closely monitored. No significant hypertension.

- Mild anemia (Grade 1, Hgb 11.8): Mild decline from baseline 13.2 g/dL, likely multifactorial: ADT-related suppression of erythropoiesis and anemia of chronic disease. Iron studies will be checked at next visit to exclude iron deficiency, particularly given prednisone-associated GI effects. No transfusion indicated at this time.

- Glucose management: HbA1c mildly above goal at 7.6%, likely exacerbated by prednisone. Will communicate with primary care physician for consideration of dose adjustment of metformin. Patient counseled on dietary management and monitoring.

- Bone health: Patient has been initiated on calcium and vitamin D supplementation. Bone mineral density (DEXA) scan ordered -- baseline BMD assessment warranted given ADT initiation and preexisting skeletal metastases. Zoledronic acid will be discussed at future visit as bone-directed therapy in context of ongoing disease.

3. GERMLINE BRCA2 -- GENETIC COUNSELING IN PROGRESS
Patient has met with genetic counselor. Family members (children, siblings) informed of autosomal dominant inheritance implications and offered cascade testing. Patient verbalized understanding of implications for future treatment eligibility, including PARP inhibitor therapy (olaparib) if disease progresses to castration-resistant state.

---

PLAN:

1. CONTINUE abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally daily on empty stomach + prednisone 5 mg BID. Remind patient about fasting requirement. Counsel to take morning prednisone before getting out of bed to minimize nausea risk.

2. CONTINUE leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months -- second injection administered today. Next injection due in approximately 3 months.

3. CONTINUE antihypertensives: lisinopril 5 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily. Recheck BP at next visit.

4. CONTINUE metformin 1000 mg BID. Will message PCP regarding HbA1c 7.6% and prednisone contribution; consider uptitration or addition of second agent if glycemic control worsens.

5. CONTINUE calcium carbonate 1200 mg daily and Vitamin D3 2000 IU daily for bone health on ADT.

6. ORDER bone mineral density (DEXA) scan -- baseline assessment given ADT initiation.

7. ORDER iron studies (serum iron, TIBC, ferritin) to evaluate mild anemia at next visit.

8. PLAN restaging bone scan and CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis at approximately 6 months of treatment (approximately 3 months from today) to formally assess imaging response.

9. PSA AND TESTOSTERONE monitoring at each follow-up visit (every 3 months).

10. Continue monitoring for mineralocorticoid excess (electrolytes, blood pressure, fluid status) at each visit.

11. Hot flash management: Discussed venlafaxine 37.5 mg daily as an option if behavioral measures fail; patient will trial non-pharmacologic approaches for 4-6 weeks first.

12. REFER to cancer rehabilitation / supervised exercise program for fatigue management.

13. FOLLOW UP in 3 months, or sooner if any new symptoms (particularly new bone pain, neurologic symptoms, or significant side effects).

Time spent in visit: approximately 45 minutes. Greater than 50% of time spent in counseling and care coordination regarding treatment goals, side effect management, and genetic implications.

Cancer status at this visit: Disease responding. Excellent biochemical response to ADT + abiraterone at 3 months.
